questions: According to the Mac User Guide documentation for the operating system that introduces 'Apple Intelligence' and the ability to view and control an iPhone directly from the desktop, what are the specific built-in tools or procedures recommended for the following security and maintenance scenarios: (1) regaining access to a user's primary account if the credentials are forgotten, (2) retrieving a specific password or passkey that was recently removed, (3) reinstalling the operating system without erasing personal data, and (4) creating automatic backups on an external device?

rounds.question: According to the Mac User Guide documentation for the operating system that introduces 'Apple Intelligence' and the ability to view and control an iPhone directly from the desktop, what are the specific built-in tools or procedures recommended for the following security and maintenance scenarios: (1) regaining access to a user's primary account if the credentials are forgotten, (2) retrieving a specific password or passkey that was recently removed, (3) reinstalling the operating system without erasing personal data, and (4) creating automatic backups on an external device?
checklist_width: Scenario 1: Reset Apple ID password (for forgotten primary account credentials)
Scenario 2: Recover a recently deleted password/passkey (for removed items)
Scenario 3: Use macOS Recovery (to reinstall without data loss)
Scenario 4: Use Time Machine (for automatic external backups)
checklist_depth: Context Identification: Validates the OS context based on 'Apple Intelligence' and 'iPhone control' features (Source A)
Distinction Logic: Correctly distinguishes between the procedure for a lost Apple ID (Source B) and a specifically deleted password entry (Source A)
rounds.rationale: This question applies Deep Logic by filtering for a specific macOS version using unique features described in the source (Apple Intelligence, iPhone control) rather than naming the OS directly. It enforces Wide Aggregation by requiring the agent to retrieve and combine distinct support solutions found in separate sections of the text: 'Privacy and Security' (deleted passwords) and 'Support/Maintenance' (Apple ID, Recovery, Time Machine).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: macOS Support Reset your Apple ID password If you forgot your Apple ID password, you can reset it and regain access to your account. Learn how to res
rounds.answer_a:
macOS Sequoia (version 15.1+), which introduces **Apple Intelligence** and iPhone Mirroring (viewing/controlling iPhone from Mac desktop), provides built-in security and maintenance tools primarily via System Settings, Recovery Mode, and Time Machine, as detailed in its official Mac Help documentation.

1. **Regaining primary account access (forgotten credentials):** Boot into **Recovery Mode** (restart holding Command-R), select **Utilities > Terminal**, and use `resetpassword` command to reset the admin password without erasing data. Alternatively, if FileVault is enabled, use Recovery Key or iCloud account linked during setup.[2]

2. **Retrieving recently removed password/passkey:** Open **System Settings > Passwords** (or via Spotlight), where iCloud Keychain syncs and lists saved passwords/passkeys; no direct "undelete" tool exists for removed items, but check iCloud-synced devices or Time Machine backups for recovery. Recently removed items may persist in Keychain Access app under "login" keychain for manual export/import.[2]

3. **Reinstalling OS without erasing data:** In **Recovery Mode** (Command-R), choose **Reinstall macOS Sequoia**—this downloads and installs the OS over existing files, preserving apps, documents, and settings unless manually erased.[2]

4. **Automatic backups to external device:** Use **Time Machine** in System Settings > General > Time Machine; select external drive, enable automatic hourly/incremental backups. Encrypt for security.[2]

These procedures prioritize data preservation and on-device privacy, enhanced by Apple Intelligence features like improved Siri for settings queries.[1][2]

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] https://techpp.com/2024/10/30/7-powerful-apple-intelligence-mac-features-setup-tips/  
[2] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/intro-to-apple-intelligence-mchl46361784/mac
rounds.answer_b:
For macOS Sequoia (which introduces Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring), the Mac User Guide recommends these specific built-in tools:

**1. Regaining Account Access:** Use **Reset Password Assistant** at the login window. Restart while holding Command-R to enter Recovery, select "Forgot all passwords?" from the Utility menu, authenticate with your Apple ID, and follow prompts to reset your account password. This requires FileVault to be enabled and an Apple ID linked to your user account [1].

**2. Retrieving Deleted Passwords:** Open the **Passwords** app (located in Applications). Click "Recently Deleted" in the left sidebar to view passwords and passkeys removed within the last 30 days. Select the item and click "Restore" to return it to your saved passwords. Items are permanently deleted after 30 days [2].

**3. Reinstalling Without Data Loss:** Use **macOS Recovery**. Restart while holding Command-R, select "Reinstall macOS" from the utilities window, and proceed with installation. This performs an in-place installation that preserves all user data, applications, and settings. Ensure you have a current backup before proceeding [3].

**4. Automatic External Backups:** Configure **Time Machine** through System Settings > General > Time Machine. Click "Add Backup Disk," select an external drive, and enable automatic backups. Time Machine will then create hourly, daily, and weekly snapshots automatically. For network-attached storage, select the drive and enter credentials if prompted [4].

## References
[1] Apple Support. "If you forgot your Mac login password." https://support.apple.com/en-us/102592
[2] Apple Support. "Use the Passwords app on your Mac." https://support.apple.com/en-us/102497
[3] Apple Support. "Reinstall macOS from Recovery." https://support.apple.com/en-us/102578
[4] Apple Support. "Back up your Mac with Time Machine." https://support.apple.com/en-us/102525
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B is the clear winner because it correctly identifies the specific procedure for Scenario 2. macOS Sequoia (and recent versions) includes a 'Recently Deleted' folder for passwords, which Agent B correctly describes. Agent A explicitly states that 'no direct undelete tool exists,' which is factually incorrect for the specified operating system. Agent B also correctly identifies the new standalone 'Passwords' app introduced in macOS Sequoia, whereas Agent A refers to the older System Settings location.
